mission.title: Install and Configure Kserve on Kubernetes
mission.description: KServe is a standardized distributed generative and predictive AI inference platform for scalable, multi-framework deployment on Kubernetes. Installing KServe allows you to leverage advanced AI capabilities in your Kubernetes environment, enabling efficient model serving and inference.

steps.title: Add the KServe Helm repository
steps.description:
First, add the KServe Helm chart repository to your Helm configuration using the following command:
```bash
helm repo add kserve https://charts.kserve.io
helm repo update
```

steps.title: Install KServe using Helm
steps.description:
Now, install KServe with the following command, creating a namespace called 'kserve-system':
```bash
helm install kserve kserve/kserve-crd-minimal --namespace kserve-system --create-namespace --version v0.16.0
```

steps.title: Verify the installation
steps.description:
Check that the KServe components are running by executing:
```bash
kubectl get pods --namespace kserve-system
```

steps.title: Configure resource limits for KServe
steps.description:
To ensure KServe operates efficiently, apply resource limits by editing the KServe deployment:
```bash
kubectl edit deployment kserve --namespace kserve-system
``` 
Add resource limits under the containers section:
```yaml
resources:
  limits:
    cpu: 500m
    memory: 512Mi
  requests:
    cpu: 250m
    memory: 256Mi
```

steps.title: Set up monitoring for KServe
steps.description:
To monitor KServe, you can integrate it with Prometheus. First, ensure Prometheus is installed in your cluster, then annotate the KServe services:
```bash
kubectl annotate service kserve --namespace kserve-system prometheus.io/scrape=true
kubectl annotate service kserve --namespace kserve-system prometheus.io/port=8080
```

resolution.summary: A successful installation of KServe will show all pods in the 'kserve-system' namespace as running. You will also have configured resource limits and set up monitoring for your KServe deployment.
codeSnippets:
helm repo add kserve https://charts.kserve.io
helm repo update
helm install kserve kserve/kserve-crd-minimal --namespace kserve-system --create-namespace --version v0.16.0

resources:
  limits:
    cpu: 500m
    memory: 512Mi
  requests:
    cpu: 250m
    memory: 256Mi

uninstall.title: Remove the Helm release
uninstall.description:
To cleanly remove the KServe installation, execute the following command to uninstall the Helm release from the 'kserve-system' namespace:
```bash
helm uninstall kserve --namespace kserve-system
```

uninstall.title: Clean up CRDs and persistent resources
uninstall.description:
After uninstalling the Helm release, remove the Custom Resource Definitions (CRDs) and any persistent resources that were created. Run the following commands:
```bash
kubectl delete crd inferenceservices.kserve.io
kubectl delete crd predictors.kserve.io
kubectl delete crd transformers.kserve.io
```

uninstall.title: Remove namespace and verify
uninstall.description:
Finally, delete the 'kserve-system' namespace to clean up any remaining resources. Verify that the namespace has been removed:
```bash
kubectl delete namespace kserve-system
kubectl get namespaces
```

upgrade.title: Backup current state
upgrade.description:
Before upgrading KServe, it's important to back up the current state of your deployment. You can do this by exporting the current configuration:
```bash
kubectl get all --namespace kserve-system -o yaml > kserve-backup.yaml
```

upgrade.title: Update repo and run upgrade command
upgrade.description:
Update the Helm repository and upgrade KServe to the latest version. Run the following commands:
```bash
helm repo update
helm upgrade kserve kserve/kserve-crd-minimal --namespace kserve-system --version v0.17.0
```

upgrade.title: Verify the upgrade
upgrade.description:
After the upgrade, check that the KServe components are running with the updated version:
```bash
kubectl get pods --namespace kserve-system
```

troubleshooting.title: Pods stuck in CrashLoopBackOff
troubleshooting.description:
If KServe pods are in a CrashLoopBackOff state, check the logs for the specific pod to diagnose the issue:
```bash
kubectl logs <pod-name> --namespace kserve-system
``` 
Look for error messages that indicate what might be causing the crash.

troubleshooting.title: Service not reachable
troubleshooting.description:
If the KServe service is not reachable, verify that the service is running and check the endpoints:
```bash
kubectl get svc --namespace kserve-system
kubectl describe svc kserve --namespace kserve-system
``` 
Ensure that the service has valid endpoints.

troubleshooting.title: Insufficient resources allocated
troubleshooting.description:
If KServe is failing to start due to insufficient resources, check the resource requests and limits set in the deployment:
```bash
kubectl get deployment kserve --namespace kserve-system -o yaml
``` 
Adjust the resource limits as necessary in the deployment configuration.

troubleshooting.title: CRDs not found
troubleshooting.description:
If you encounter issues related to missing CRDs, ensure that the CRDs were installed correctly. You can check the status of the CRDs with:
```bash
kubectl get crd
``` 
If they are missing, reinstall the CRDs using the Helm chart.

prerequisites.description: Ensure you have a Kubernetes cluster running version 1.24 or higher, and have Helm and kubectl installed on your local machine.
